- [Schema Markup Hotels — Travel Tractions](https://traveltractions.com/schema-markup-for-hotels/)

Synthesis Note
OTA leaders and major chains are racing to embed themselves in AI distribution ecosystems. Radisson must match schema implementation depth and FAQ/conversational content quality to compete for AI citation share.

---

Q5: Content Patterns for American Bleisure Travelers Using AI Search

Dated Findings
2025–2026 — Bleisure traveler AI search behavior:

- Definition: Bleisure = business + leisure; ages 25–55, US-based, traveling to Europe, increasingly using AI as primary hotel discovery interface
- AI adoption rate: 15% of Americans use AI for trip planning (Deloitte 2025, up from 10% in 2024); 83% have used or are interested in AI travel tools (2026 TravelBoom Leisure Travel Study)
- Query evolution: From generic ("best hotels in Paris") to highly specific, conversational queries ("I'm traveling to Paris in summer with two kids—what area and which hotel would you recommend?")
- Bleisure-specific query patterns likely include:
  - "hotel in [city] with meeting rooms and good WiFi for business travelers"
  - "best hotel in London near financial district for American executives"
  - "hotels with leisure amenities after business travel in Amsterdam"
  - "Radisson in [city] — is it good for American business travelers?"
  - "hotel with rooftop bar and spa near [European city center]"
- Booking via AI: 46% of AI travel users use it to explore accommodation; 42% have booked based on AI recommendations in 2025
- Platform preferences: ChatGPT for complex, multi-day scaffolding; Perplexity for research with citations; Google AI Overviews for quick comparisons

- [Tripadvisor and ChatGPT for Travel — Travel Weekly](https://www.travelweekly.com/Travel-News/Travel-Technology/Tripadvisor-says-ChatGPT-app-revolutionize-travel-planning)

Synthesis Note
American bleisure travelers are now formulating AI queries with business AND leisure dimensions simultaneously. Hotel pages must answer compound, conversational queries that include location, amenity type, traveler profile, and trip purpose — not just destination.

---

Cross-Run Comparison
First run — no prior run for comparison. All findings represent the fresh baseline.

Key Shifts Detected (vs. implicit prior assumptions)
1. AI booking has arrived: Perplexity and ChatGPT now support in-app hotel booking (2025). Visibility in AI = direct bookings, not just discovery.
2. FAQ schema is the highest-ROI single action: 3x citation boost documented. Previously viewed as "nice to have."
3. OTAs are moving into AI ecosystems as distribution channels: Tripadvisor/Perplexity partnership, Expedia/Copilot integration. Hotel brands without direct AI presence lose to OTAs even more severely than in traditional SEO.
4. Content freshness now measurably decays in 3–5 day cycles: This changes the content maintenance calculus significantly.
5. Zero-click is now the majority: 69% of Google searches result in zero clicks (2025). AI answer optimization is now the primary discovery battleground.

Q1: Which AI Engines Are Currently Dominant for Travel and Hotel Discovery?

Dated Findings
March 2026 — Market leadership by platform:

- Google Gemini / Google AI Overviews: Google AI Overviews now appear in >30% of queries (up from 6.49% in Jan 2025). Highly trusted for real-time integration with Google Flights and Google Hotels. YouTube and multi-modal content preferred (23.3% of citations).
- Perplexity AI: 18–22% of AI chatbot referral traffic. Launched hotel booking directly in-app (March 2025, via Selfbook + Tripadvisor partnership). Heavily cites Reddit content (46.7%). Partnered with Tripadvisor for trip planning.
- Microsoft Copilot: 6–9% of AI chatbot referral traffic. Integrated with Expedia for agentic hotel/ticket booking.
- Emerging: Claude (Anthropic), Deepseek also present but smaller market share in travel context.

Source Links
- [AI Chatbot Market Share 2026 — Vertu/Similarweb](https://vertu.com/lifestyle/ai-chatbot-market-share-2026-chatgpt-drops-to-68-as-google-gemini-surges-to-18-2/)

Synthesis Note
Schema is no longer optional — it is the primary machine interface for AI engine understanding. FAQ schema + LodgingBusiness + quick-answer blocks form the critical triad for hotel discoverability.

---

Q3: Current Best-Evidenced GEO and AEO Practices for Hospitality

Dated Findings
2025–2026 — Hospitality-specific GEO/AEO practices:

- GEO defined: Process of optimizing website content to be accurately understood, used, and prioritized by AI-driven search engines and AI agents. Goal is to become the primary recommendation within an AI-generated response, not just rank in a SERP.
- AEO defined: Optimizing content structure and topical authority to be the definitive response in AI-powered search. Key difference from SEO: zero-click answers are the target outcome.
- Zero-click growth: Zero-click searches rose from 56% (2024) to 69% (2025). AI Overviews appear in >30% of queries by late 2025.
- Content structure requirements:
  - Use FAQs, amenity descriptions, conversational language
  - Listicle-format ranking pages (74.2% AI citation rate)
  - Triple JSON-LD schema stacking: Article + ItemList + FAQPage on ranking pages
  - Quick Answer blocks in first 200 words
  - Scorecard comparison tables
- Authority signals: Reviews, consistent brand presence, third-party citations
- Topical authority: Comprehensive coverage of destination, amenity, and traveler-type content builds AI engine confidence in brand expertise
- Content decay management: Content must be refreshed to maintain citation pool presence

- [GEO for Hotels — O'Rourke Hospitality](https://www.orourkehospitality.com/insights/what-is-generative-engine-optimization-for-hotels/)

Synthesis Note
GEO and AEO have merged into a unified discipline in hospitality. The winning formula is: structured schema + FAQ blocks + conversational natural language content + topical authority + freshness maintenance.

---

Q4: What Metadata and Content Structures Are OTAs and Hospitality Leaders Using?

Dated Findings
2025–2026 — Competitive intelligence:

- Booking.com: AI Trip Planner with real-time reviews and visual itineraries. New integrations designed to keep inventory accessible within AI ecosystems ("the next API moment"). Comprehensive schema markup across property pages.
- Expedia Group: ChatGPT-powered app for conversational planning; Agentic AI via Microsoft Copilot for booking. Strong structured data practices across all inventory.
- Marriott International: RENAI AI concierge (Renaissance Hotels); AI search for Marriott Bonvoy luxury rentals. Implementing schema markup at scale.
- Hilton: Hilton AI Planner (generative AI concierge at hilton.com) providing real-time conversational responses to travel queries.
- Tripadvisor: Enhanced AI visibility via FAQ sections for destinations and hotels → more answer placements for queries like "best time to visit…" and "things to do in…"
- Industry benchmark: >72% of first-page Google results use schema markup; <30% of all websites have implemented it.

Synthesis Note
OTA leaders and major chains are racing to embed themselves in AI distribution ecosystems. Radisson must match schema implementation depth and FAQ/conversational content quality to compete for AI citation share.

---

Q5: Content Patterns for American Bleisure Travelers Using AI Search

Dated Findings
2025–2026 — Bleisure traveler AI search behavior:

- Definition: Bleisure = business + leisure; ages 25–55, US-based, traveling to Europe, increasingly using AI as primary hotel discovery interface
- AI adoption rate: 15% of Americans use AI for trip planning (Deloitte 2025, up from 10% in 2024); 83% have used or are interested in AI travel tools (2026 TravelBoom Leisure Travel Study)
- Query evolution: From generic ("best hotels in Paris") to highly specific, conversational queries ("I'm traveling to Paris in summer with two kids—what area and which hotel would you recommend?")
- Bleisure-specific query patterns likely include:
  - "hotel in [city] with meeting rooms and good WiFi for business travelers"
  - "best hotel in London near financial district for American executives"
  - "hotels with leisure amenities after business travel in Amsterdam"
  - "Radisson in [city] — is it good for American business travelers?"
  - "hotel with rooftop bar and spa near [European city center]"
- Booking via AI: 46% of AI travel users use it to explore accommodation; 42% have booked based on AI recommendations in 2025
- Platform preferences: ChatGPT for complex, multi-day scaffolding; Perplexity for research with citations; Google AI Overviews for quick comparisons

- [Tripadvisor and ChatGPT for Travel — Travel Weekly](https://www.travelweekly.com/Travel-News/Travel-Technology/Tripadvisor-says-ChatGPT-app-revolutionize-travel-planning)

Synthesis Note
American bleisure travelers are now formulating AI queries with business AND leisure dimensions simultaneously. Hotel pages must answer compound, conversational queries that include location, amenity type, traveler profile, and trip purpose — not just destination.

---

Cross-Run Comparison
First run — no prior run for comparison. All findings represent the fresh baseline.

Key Shifts Detected (vs. implicit prior assumptions)
1. AI booking has arrived: Perplexity and ChatGPT now support in-app hotel booking (2025). Visibility in AI = direct bookings, not just discovery.
2. FAQ schema is the highest-ROI single action: 3x citation boost documented. Previously viewed as "nice to have."
3. OTAs are moving into AI ecosystems as distribution channels: Tripadvisor/Perplexity partnership, Expedia/Copilot integration. Hotel brands without direct AI presence lose to OTAs even more severely than in traditional SEO.
4. Content freshness now measurably decays in 3–5 day cycles: This changes the content maintenance calculus significantly.
5. Zero-click is now the majority: 69% of Google searches result in zero clicks (2025). AI answer optimization is now the primary discovery battleground.

Literature Findings — run_001 | 2026-03-20

Research Context
Fresh web research conducted on 2026-03-20 for GEO/AEO environment relevant to Radisson Hotel Group's discoverability by American bleisure travelers (ages 25–55, US→Europe, AI-first discovery).

Q1: Which AI Engines Are Currently Dominant for Travel and Hotel Discovery?

Dated Findings
March 2026 — Market leadership by platform:

- ChatGPT (OpenAI): 80.49% AI chatbot market share; 883M monthly users; 5.4B global monthly visits. Primary platform for natural language travel planning and "defensible" itineraries. ChatGPT confirmed use of structured data to determine which products appear in results.
- Google Gemini / Google AI Overviews: Google AI Overviews now appear in >30% of queries (up from 6.49% in Jan 2025). Highly trusted for real-time integration with Google Flights and Google Hotels. YouTube and multi-modal content preferred (23.3% of citations).
- Perplexity AI: 18–22% of AI chatbot referral traffic. Launched hotel booking directly in-app (March 2025, via Selfbook + Tripadvisor partnership). Heavily cites Reddit content (46.7%). Partnered with Tripadvisor for trip planning.
- Microsoft Copilot: 6–9% of AI chatbot referral traffic. Integrated with Expedia for agentic hotel/ticket booking.
- Emerging: Claude (Anthropic), Deepseek also present but smaller market share in travel context.

Source Links
- [AI Chatbot Market Share 2026 — Vertu/Similarweb](https://vertu.com/lifestyle/ai-chatbot-market-share-2026-chatgpt-drops-to-68-as-google-gemini-surges-to-18-2/)
- [AI Traffic Share Report 2026 — upGrowth](https://upgrowth.in/ai-traffic-share-report-2026/)
- [ChatGPT & Google Gemini Tops for AI Travel Planning — Chris Cruises](https://www.chriscruises.com/chatgpt-google-gemini-tops-for-ai-travel-planning/)
- [ChatGPT vs. Perplexity vs. Google AI Mode Citation Benchmarks 2026 — Averi AI](https://www.averi.ai/how-to/chatgpt-vs.-perplexity-vs.-google-ai-mode-the-b2b-saas-citation-benchmarks-report-(2026))

- [Tripadvisor partners with Perplexity — PhocusWire](https://www.phocuswire.com/tripadvisor-perplexity-ai-partnership)

Synthesis Note
The "big three" for hotel discovery are ChatGPT, Google AI Overviews, and Perplexity. Each has distinct content preferences. Brand pages must serve structured, machine-readable, authoritative content for all three simultaneously.

---

Q2: What Signals Do Dominant AI Engines Use to Surface Hotel Results?

Dated Findings
2025–2026 signal evidence:

- Structured data (Schema.org): Google and Microsoft publicly confirm use of Schema Markup for generative AI features. ChatGPT confirmed structured data determines product appearance. AI systems parse at scale and prioritize machine-readable context over visually appealing pages.
- FAQ schema: Single most impactful structured data type for AI citations in 2026. Pages with 5–8 well-implemented FAQ schema Q&A pairs cited up to 3x more than equivalent pages without FAQ schema.
- Listicle / "Top N" format: 74.2% of all AI citations come from structured "Top N" content. AI engines favor numbered, rankable lists.
- Quick Answer blocks: First 200 words must contain extractable ranked summaries. Above-the-fold placement strongly preferred.
- Reviews and trust signals: AI systems analyze community discussion and review sentiment to assess trust and increase recommendation probability.
- Content freshness: New content enters AI citation pools within 3–5 business days. Content decays without freshness updates.
- Entity recognition: Named brand + location + category + service type signals required for confident AI entity resolution.
- Multi-modal content: Google AI Overviews prefer YouTube and visual content (23.3% citation share).

Synthesis Note

Synthesis Note
GEO and AEO have merged into a unified discipline in hospitality. The winning formula is: structured schema + FAQ blocks + conversational natural language content + topical authority + freshness maintenance.

---

Q4: What Metadata and Content Structures Are OTAs and Hospitality Leaders Using?

Dated Findings
2025–2026 — Competitive intelligence:

- Booking.com: AI Trip Planner with real-time reviews and visual itineraries. New integrations designed to keep inventory accessible within AI ecosystems ("the next API moment"). Comprehensive schema markup across property pages.
- Expedia Group: ChatGPT-powered app for conversational planning; Agentic AI via Microsoft Copilot for booking. Strong structured data practices across all inventory.
- Marriott International: RENAI AI concierge (Renaissance Hotels); AI search for Marriott Bonvoy luxury rentals. Implementing schema markup at scale.
- Hilton: Hilton AI Planner (generative AI concierge at hilton.com) providing real-time conversational responses to travel queries.
- Tripadvisor: Enhanced AI visibility via FAQ sections for destinations and hotels → more answer placements for queries like "best time to visit…" and "things to do in…"
- Industry benchmark: >72% of first-page Google results use schema markup; <30% of all websites have implemented it.

Source Links
- [Direct Booking 2026: Personalization and AI Visibility — Hospitality.today](https://www.hospitality.today/article/direct-booking-in-2026-personalization-ai-visibility-and-experience)
- [Inside the AI Booking Engine — Westlight Marketing](https://www.westlightmarketing.com/blog/ai-booking-engine-travel-mcp)
- [Hilton AI Planner — TheStreet](https://www.thestreet.com/travel/hilton-rolls-out-new-ai-planner-as-travelers-plan-trips-differently)
- [What Direct AI Hotel Distribution Looks Like — Hospitality.today](https://www.hospitality.today/article/what-direct-ai-hotel-distribution-looks-like)
- [Schema Markup for Hotels — SEO Clarity](https://www.seoclarity.net/blog/the-best-schema-markup-for-hotels-and-travel-sites)
- [Schema Markup for Hotels — TravelBoom](https://www.travelboommarketing.com/blog/the-best-schema-markup-for-hotels-and-travel-sites)
- [Schema Markup Hotels — Travel Tractions](https://traveltractions.com/schema-markup-for-hotels/)

Synthesis Note
OTA leaders and major chains are racing to embed themselves in AI distribution ecosystems. Radisson must match schema implementation depth and FAQ/conversational content quality to compete for AI citation share.

---

Q5: Content Patterns for American Bleisure Travelers Using AI Search

Dated Findings
2025–2026 — Bleisure traveler AI search behavior:

- Definition: Bleisure = business + leisure; ages 25–55, US-based, traveling to Europe, increasingly using AI as primary hotel discovery interface
- AI adoption rate: 15% of Americans use AI for trip planning (Deloitte 2025, up from 10% in 2024); 83% have used or are interested in AI travel tools (2026 TravelBoom Leisure Travel Study)
- Query evolution: From generic ("best hotels in Paris") to highly specific, conversational queries ("I'm traveling to Paris in summer with two kids—what area and which hotel would you recommend?")
- Bleisure-specific query patterns likely include:
  - "hotel in [city] with meeting rooms and good WiFi for business travelers"
  - "best hotel in London near financial district for American executives"
  - "hotels with leisure amenities after business travel in Amsterdam"
  - "Radisson in [city] — is it good for American business travelers?"
  - "hotel with rooftop bar and spa near [European city center]"
- Booking via AI: 46% of AI travel users use it to explore accommodation; 42% have booked based on AI recommendations in 2025
- Platform preferences: ChatGPT for complex, multi-day scaffolding; Perplexity for research with citations; Google AI Overviews for quick comparisons

Source Links
- [How AI Search Reshapes Hotel Discovery — DialogShift](https://www.dialogshift.com/en/blog-posts/die-evolution-der-websuche-wie-ki-die-spielregeln-verandert)
- [Perplexity AI Hotel Search Agent — Triptease](https://www.triptease.com/resources/perplexity-ai-hotel-search-agent)
- [5 Steps to Optimize Hotel Website for AI Search — HospitalityNet](https://www.hospitalitynet.org/news/4128636.html)
- [Hotels Push Live ChatGPT Integrations — Hotel Investment Today](https://www.hotelinvestmenttoday.com/Asset-Management/Hotels-push-live-ChatGPT-integrations-as-AI-search-goes-bookable)
- [Tripadvisor and ChatGPT for Travel — Travel Weekly](https://www.travelweekly.com/Travel-News/Travel-Technology/Tripadvisor-says-ChatGPT-app-revolutionize-travel-planning)

Synthesis Note
American bleisure travelers are now formulating AI queries with business AND leisure dimensions simultaneously. Hotel pages must answer compound, conversational queries that include location, amenity type, traveler profile, and trip purpose — not just destination.

---

Cross-Run Comparison
First run — no prior run for comparison. All findings represent the fresh baseline.

Key Shifts Detected (vs. implicit prior assumptions)
1. AI booking has arrived: Perplexity and ChatGPT now support in-app hotel booking (2025). Visibility in AI = direct bookings, not just discovery.
2. FAQ schema is the highest-ROI single action: 3x citation boost documented. Previously viewed as "nice to have."
3. OTAs are moving into AI ecosystems as distribution channels: Tripadvisor/Perplexity partnership, Expedia/Copilot integration. Hotel brands without direct AI presence lose to OTAs even more severely than in traditional SEO.
4. Content freshness now measurably decays in 3–5 day cycles: This changes the content maintenance calculus significantly.
5. Zero-click is now the majority: 69% of Google searches result in zero clicks (2025). AI answer optimization is now the primary discovery battleground.

criteria.title: LodgingBusiness / Hotel Schema Presence
criteria.signal_name: Schema.org LodgingBusiness or Hotel structured data
criteria.raw_excerpt:
## C01 — LodgingBusiness / Hotel Schema Presence

**Signal name:** Schema.org LodgingBusiness or Hotel structured data
**Why it matters for AI hotel discovery:** Google and Microsoft explicitly use Schema.org structured data for generative AI features. ChatGPT confirmed it uses structured data to determine which products appear in results. AI systems parse schema at scale and prioritize machine-readable context over unstructured text. Without it, the page is effectively invisible to AI parsing layers.
**What "passing" looks like:** Page contains valid JSON-LD block with `@type: "Hotel"` or `@type: "LodgingBusiness"` including at minimum: name, address (PostalAddress), telephone, image, description, checkinTime, checkoutTime, amenityFeature, starRating, and url.
**How to check during audit:** View page source or use browser DevTools → inspect `<script type="application/ld+json">` blocks. Validate against schema.org/Hotel. Check for nested amenityFeature, starRating, geo coordinates, and priceRange fields.
**Relevance to American bleisure traveler queries:** When a bleisure traveler asks ChatGPT "best Radisson in London with meeting rooms," structured data is how Radisson's meeting room amenity is surfaced as a parseable feature rather than buried in paragraph text.

---

criteria.title: FAQPage Schema with Conversational Query Coverage
criteria.signal_name: FAQPage structured data aligned to natural language traveler queries
criteria.raw_excerpt:
## C02 — FAQPage Schema with Conversational Query Coverage

**Signal name:** FAQPage structured data aligned to natural language traveler queries
**Why it matters for AI hotel discovery:** FAQ schema is the single most impactful structured data type for AI citations in 2026. Pages with 5–8 well-implemented Q&A pairs get cited up to 3x more than equivalent pages without FAQ schema. AI engines treat FAQ blocks as pre-extracted answers ready for citation.
**What "passing" looks like:** Page contains JSON-LD `@type: "FAQPage"` with 5–8 Q&A pairs. Questions must use natural language matching real traveler queries (not internal marketing language). At least 2–3 questions must address the bleisure traveler profile (e.g., "Do Radisson Blu hotels have business centers?", "Which Radisson hotels in London are near the financial district?").
**How to check during audit:** Inspect JSON-LD blocks for FAQPage type. Also check visible FAQ or Q&A sections on page rendered HTML. Evaluate question phrasing for conversational vs. marketing register.
**Relevance to American bleisure traveler queries:** Bleisure travelers use highly specific, conversational AI queries. FAQ schema ensures Radisson pages contain pre-formatted answers that AI engines extract and cite directly, rather than paraphrasing competitor content.

---

criteria.title: Title Tag: Conversational Query Alignment
criteria.signal_name: Title tag containing traveler-intent keywords and brand entity
criteria.raw_excerpt:
## C03 — Title Tag: Conversational Query Alignment

**Signal name:** Title tag containing traveler-intent keywords and brand entity
**Why it matters for AI hotel discovery:** Title tags remain a primary entity signal for both traditional search and AI citation selection. AI engines use titles to understand page scope and match it against query intent. Generic titles ("Radisson Hotels") fail to match specific conversational queries.
**What "passing" looks like:** Title includes brand name (Radisson / Radisson Blu / Park Plaza), city or region, and one traveler-intent modifier (e.g., "luxury," "business travelers," "Europe hotels"). Format: `[Brand] [City] Hotels | [Intent Modifier]`. Character length 50–65.
**How to check during audit:** Extract `<title>` tag from page source. Evaluate brand presence, geographic specificity, and intent alignment. Check character count.
**Relevance to American bleisure traveler queries:** American travelers searching for "Radisson hotels London business" need the title to match their query scope. Misaligned titles reduce citation probability.

---

criteria.title: Meta Description: Natural Language Answer Preview
criteria.signal_name: Meta description structured as a natural language answer to traveler queries
criteria.raw_excerpt:
## C04 — Meta Description: Natural Language Answer Preview

**Signal name:** Meta description structured as a natural language answer to traveler queries
**Why it matters for AI hotel discovery:** Meta descriptions function as answer previews. AI engines scan meta descriptions to assess whether the page likely contains the answer to a user query. A description written as a statement ("Discover luxury Radisson hotels…") scores lower than one written as an answer ("Radisson hotels in London offer business-class amenities, central locations near Canary Wharf, and flexible stay options for American business travelers.").
**What "passing" looks like:** Meta description 140–160 characters, structured as a declarative sentence answering "what does this hotel offer and to whom." Includes brand, city, at least one amenity, and one traveler-type signal. No marketing fluff verbs ("Discover," "Experience," "Explore").
**How to check during audit:** Extract `<meta name="description">` from page source. Parse for query-answer format vs. marketing CTA format. Check length.
**Relevance to American bleisure traveler queries:** Bleisure travelers use natural language AI queries. Meta descriptions that read as answers increase the probability of AI engines selecting this page as the citation source.

---

criteria.title: Open Graph Tags: Social and AI Preview Accuracy
criteria.signal_name: Open Graph metadata (og:title, og:description, og:image, og:type)
criteria.raw_excerpt:
## C05 — Open Graph Tags: Social and AI Preview Accuracy

**Signal name:** Open Graph metadata (og:title, og:description, og:image, og:type)
**Why it matters for AI hotel discovery:** Open Graph tags are used by AI engines and social platforms to understand page identity when fetching content for citation. Missing or mismatched OG tags reduce confidence in page identity, especially for brand pages. Perplexity and other engines fetch OG data as part of citation evaluation.
**What "passing" looks like:** Page contains `og:title`, `og:description`, `og:image` (with hotel photography, not generic placeholders), `og:type: "website"` or `og:type: "place"`, and `og:url` matching canonical URL. og:description should match or complement the meta description in natural language format.
**How to check during audit:** Inspect page source for `<meta property="og:*">` tags. Check image URL resolves to real hotel photography. Confirm og:url matches page URL.
**Relevance to American bleisure traveler queries:** When Perplexity or ChatGPT fetch a Radisson page to evaluate it as a citation, Open Graph data is part of the content fingerprint. Accurate OG data increases citation confidence.

---

criteria.title: Heading Structure: Scannable and Query-Aligned
criteria.signal_name: H1/H2/H3 hierarchy aligned to traveler query vocabulary
criteria.raw_excerpt:
## C06 — Heading Structure: Scannable and Query-Aligned

**Signal name:** H1/H2/H3 hierarchy aligned to traveler query vocabulary
**Why it matters for AI hotel discovery:** AI engines parse heading structures to understand page information architecture. Headings act as semantic anchors: an H2 reading "Hotels in London for Business Travelers" signals content relevance to bleisure queries far more effectively than "Our Properties."
**What "passing" looks like:** Page has exactly one H1 containing brand + location or brand + category. H2s cover distinct traveler-relevant topics (e.g., "Business Amenities," "Location and Transport," "Meeting Facilities," "Leisure After Work"). H3s add specificity within each topic. No duplicate heading levels. Headings use traveler vocabulary, not internal brand jargon.
**How to check during audit:** Extract full heading outline (H1→H3) from rendered page. Evaluate alignment to bleisure traveler query vocabulary. Check for duplicate H1s and missing H2 structure.
**Relevance to American bleisure traveler queries:** Headings that match query patterns ("London hotels near financial district," "business meeting facilities") increase AI engine confidence that the page directly addresses the traveler's question.

---

criteria.title: Entity Signals: Brand + Location + Category Clarity
criteria.signal_name: Named entity clarity — Radisson as a brand with location, category, and service type
criteria.raw_excerpt:
## C07 — Entity Signals: Brand + Location + Category Clarity

**Signal name:** Named entity clarity — Radisson as a brand with location, category, and service type
**Why it matters for AI hotel discovery:** AI engines build knowledge graphs of entities. For Radisson to appear in ChatGPT or Perplexity responses to "best European hotel brands for business travelers," the page must consistently and unambiguously signal: brand name, property category (luxury/upscale), geographic footprint (Europe), and service type (hotel/lodging/business travel).
**What "passing" looks like:** Page mentions "Radisson" as a named brand (not just implied) at least 3× in body text. Mentions at least one geographic scope (city, country, or "Europe"). Mentions at least one service category ("hotel," "business hotel," "luxury hotel"). Mentions at least one traveler type ("business travelers," "bleisure travelers," "American guests").
**How to check during audit:** Read page body content and count explicit brand, location, category, and traveler-type mentions. Check if structured data reinforces these entity signals.
**Relevance to American bleisure traveler queries:** Named entity recognition is how AI engines resolve "which Radisson hotel is best for an American in London" — the brand must be confidently recognized as an entity with known attributes.

---

criteria.title: Natural Language Query Compatibility
criteria.signal_name: Page content can directly answer a conversational AI travel query
criteria.raw_excerpt:
## C08 — Natural Language Query Compatibility

**Signal name:** Page content can directly answer a conversational AI travel query
**Why it matters for AI hotel discovery:** AI engines match user queries to content by assessing whether the page would plausibly answer the question. A page that only contains marketing copy ("Experience the Radisson Difference") cannot answer "Is Radisson Blu good for American business travelers in London?" A page that explicitly addresses this query type becomes a citation candidate.
**What "passing" looks like:** Page body text contains at least one paragraph directly addressing a realistic bleisure traveler query: traveler type (business, bleisure, American), purpose (meeting, conference, leisure extension), amenity need (WiFi, business center, gym, bar), and location context. Ideally structured as a clear statement that could be extracted as a direct answer.
**How to check during audit:** Read page body text. Identify if any passage directly answers a query like "hotels in [city] for American business travelers with meeting rooms." Note the query coverage score: 0 = no match, 1 = partial match, 2 = direct match.
**Relevance to American bleisure traveler queries:** This is the core signal. Pages that can answer the traveler's actual question will be cited; pages that cannot will be skipped, even with perfect technical schema.

---

criteria.title: Alt Text on Meaningful Images
criteria.signal_name: Descriptive alt text on hotel photography
criteria.raw_excerpt:
## C09 — Alt Text on Meaningful Images

**Signal name:** Descriptive alt text on hotel photography
**Why it matters for AI hotel discovery:** Google AI Overviews favor multi-modal content (23.3% citation share from visual content). Alt text is the primary signal for image content understanding. Hotel room, amenity, and location images with descriptive alt text contribute to entity recognition and content relevance scoring.
**What "passing" looks like:** All meaningful hotel images (rooms, lobby, pool, business center, restaurant, cityscape) have descriptive alt text including property name and content description (e.g., "Radisson Blu London Executive Floor Business Room with city views"). No empty alt attributes on meaningful images. Decorative images may have empty alt.
**How to check during audit:** Inspect image `alt` attributes in rendered page source. Sample 5–10 meaningful images per page. Check for empty, generic ("image1.jpg"), or keyword-stuffed alt text.
**Relevance to American bleisure traveler queries:** Google AI Overviews with visual components increasingly appear for hotel discovery queries. Proper alt text ensures Radisson images are included in multi-modal AI responses.

---

criteria.title: Content Freshness Signals
criteria.raw_excerpt:
## C10 — Content Freshness Signals

**Signal name:** Visible or schema-declared content freshness
**Why it matters for AI hotel discovery:** New content enters AI citation pools within 3–5 business days; content decays and loses citation priority without freshness updates. AI engines weight recently updated content higher in citation selection for time-sensitive topics like hotel availability, pricing, and amenities.
**What "passing" looks like:** Page contains a visible "Last updated" date, OR schema dateModified field in structured data, OR recent blog/news content linked from page. Content references current year (2025 or 2026). No outdated information visible (old promotions, past events, pre-2024 pricing ranges).
**How to check during audit:** Check for dateModified in JSON-LD. Check for visible date stamps on content. Check whether body text references current-year context. Note any obviously stale content.
**Relevance to American bleisure traveler queries:** Travelers using AI engines expect current information. Stale content reduces citation confidence and may cause AI engines to prefer fresher OTA pages for the same query.

---

criteria.title: Amenity Coverage: Bleisure-Specific Features
criteria.signal_name: Explicit coverage of business AND leisure amenities for the bleisure profile
criteria.raw_excerpt:
## C11 — Amenity Coverage: Bleisure-Specific Features

**Signal name:** Explicit coverage of business AND leisure amenities for the bleisure profile
**Why it matters for AI hotel discovery:** Bleisure travelers issue compound queries combining business and leisure needs. Pages that only mention one dimension (business center OR spa, not both) fail to match the compound query. AI engines parse amenity mentions to assess relevance to multi-dimensional traveler needs.
**What "passing" looks like:** Page explicitly mentions both business amenities (meeting rooms, business center, high-speed WiFi, flexible check-in/out, airport transfer) AND leisure amenities (restaurant, bar, spa, gym, pool, city location for tourism). Ideally mentions "bleisure," "work and leisure," or equivalent phrasing.
**How to check during audit:** Scan body text and structured data amenityFeature list. Catalog all business amenities mentioned and all leisure amenities mentioned. Score: 0 = neither, 1 = one dimension, 2 = both dimensions.
**Relevance to American bleisure traveler queries:** This is the defining signal for bleisure discoverability. A page scoring 2 on amenity coverage can match queries like "hotel with conference room and great bar in Amsterdam" — a compound query that is increasingly common among AI-assisted bleisure planners.

---

criteria.title: Geographic Entity Signals for European Destinations
criteria.signal_name: Geographic specificity for European cities relevant to US→Europe bleisure travel
criteria.raw_excerpt:
## C12 — Geographic Entity Signals for European Destinations

**Signal name:** Geographic specificity for European cities relevant to US→Europe bleisure travel
**Why it matters for AI hotel discovery:** American bleisure travelers traveling to Europe use city-specific and region-specific queries. AI engines need to resolve "Radisson" to specific European geographic entities to answer "Which Radisson hotel is near Heathrow?" or "Radisson Amsterdam near the business district." Generic Europe-level content scores lower than city-specific content.
**What "passing" looks like:** Page (for city-specific pages) mentions the specific city by name ≥3×; mentions neighborhood or district context (e.g., "near Canary Wharf," "in the 8th arrondissement," "steps from Central Station"); mentions proximity to relevant US traveler reference points (airport, financial district, major attractions). Country mentioned at least once.
**How to check during audit:** For city-specific pages, count city name mentions. Check for district/neighborhood specificity. Check for proximity signals to US-traveler-known landmarks or transport hubs. For brand overview pages, check for country/region coverage breadth.
**Relevance to American bleisure traveler queries:** American travelers often navigate European cities by airport, financial district, and iconic landmark — not local neighborhood names. Pages that reference these entry-point geographies match the mental model of the target traveler.

---

recommendations.title: Audit Radisson.com robots.txt for AI Crawler Blocking (GAP-017 + systemic)
recommendations.current_state: All direct HTTP fetches of radissonhotels.com returned 403. This may indicate user-agent-based blocking, IP-range blocking, or JavaScript rendering dependency. Whether AI crawler agents (GPTBot, PerplexityBot) are also blocked is unconfirmed.
recommendations.proposed_change: Audit radissonhotels.com robots.txt and meta robots configurations to confirm that legitimate AI crawlers — GPTBot (ChatGPT), PerplexityBot, ClaudeBot, Bingbot/Copilot — are not blocked by blanket bot-blocking rules. If blocked: implement selective whitelisting for named AI crawlers while maintaining scraper/bad-bot protections. If JavaScript-only rendering is causing metadata to be inaccessible: implement server-side rendering (SSR) or static HTML export for all brand and overview pages. Confirm that User-agent: GPTBot, User-agent: PerplexityBot, and User-agent: Bingbot are permitted in robots.txt or not specifically blocked.

before_after.before: All direct HTTP fetches of radissonhotels.com returned 403. This may indicate user-agent-based blocking, IP-range blocking, or JavaScript rendering dependency. Whether AI crawler agents (GPTBot, PerplexityBot) are also blocked is unconfirmed.

If JavaScript-only rendering is causing metadata to be inaccessible: implement server-side rendering (SSR) or static HTML export for all brand and overview pages. recommendations.source_citation: C01 — Schema Presence (prerequisite). Research: Bot-blocked pages generate zero AI citations regardless of content quality. The 403 errors returned to this audit's direct fetch attempts suggest blanket blocking that may affect legitimate AI crawlers (GEO checklist, totheweb.com; assessment from gap_research.md Phase 2.5 sub-agent). Best practice for AI crawler access is actively contested — selective whitelisting is the recommended intermediate approach (Gap Research Assessment, run_001).
recommendations.impact_estimate: CRITICAL PREREQUISITE. If AI crawlers are blocked, all other GEO improvements are ineffective. Schema, FAQ content, and factual rewrites only generate AI citations if the page can be crawled. This must be confirmed before any other optimization is deployed. Best practice is contested (privacy vs. visibility tradeoff), but the baseline position should be: AI crawlers are permitted.
recommendations.raw_excerpt:
### PROP-016 — Audit Radisson.com robots.txt for AI Crawler Blocking (GAP-017 + systemic)

**1. Proposed change**
Audit radissonhotels.com `robots.txt` and `meta robots` configurations to confirm that legitimate AI crawlers — GPTBot (ChatGPT), PerplexityBot, ClaudeBot, Bingbot/Copilot — are not blocked by blanket bot-blocking rules. If blocked: implement selective whitelisting for named AI crawlers while maintaining scraper/bad-bot protections. If JavaScript-only rendering is causing metadata to be inaccessible: implement server-side rendering (SSR) or static HTML export for all brand and overview pages. Confirm that `User-agent: GPTBot`, `User-agent: PerplexityBot`, and `User-agent: Bingbot` are permitted in `robots.txt` or not specifically blocked.

**2. Source citation**
C01 — Schema Presence (prerequisite). Research: Bot-blocked pages generate zero AI citations regardless of content quality. The 403 errors returned to this audit's direct fetch attempts suggest blanket blocking that may affect legitimate AI crawlers (GEO checklist, totheweb.com; assessment from gap_research.md Phase 2.5 sub-agent). Best practice for AI crawler access is actively contested — selective whitelisting is the recommended intermediate approach (Gap Research Assessment, run_001).

**3. Current state**
All direct HTTP fetches of radissonhotels.com returned 403. This may indicate user-agent-based blocking, IP-range blocking, or JavaScript rendering dependency. Whether AI crawler agents (GPTBot, PerplexityBot) are also blocked is unconfirmed.

**4. Inferred implementation status**
N/A — first run.

**5. Directional impact estimate**
CRITICAL PREREQUISITE. If AI crawlers are blocked, all other GEO improvements are ineffective. Schema, FAQ content, and factual rewrites only generate AI citations if the page can be crawled. This must be confirmed before any other optimization is deployed. Best practice is contested (privacy vs. visibility tradeoff), but the baseline position should be: AI crawlers are permitted.

**6. Priority tier**
P1 (prerequisite)
